summary: Martin Fowler shares his first impressions of the HTC Magic Android phone after a month of use, having received it at Google IO. He explains why he moved away from his Nokia E61 and why iPhone and Blackberry weren't options for him. Overall he finds the Android device satisfactory, praising its physical design, battery life, app ecosystem, and Google service integration. However, he notes frustrations with local HTML browsing limitations, timezone handling in the calendar, and the soft keyboard compared to physical keys.
key_points: The phone's physical design, screen quality, and battery life meet his needs for mobile web browsing
Google service integration (Gmail, Calendar) works seamlessly with the device
The inability to browse local HTML files via file:// URLs is a significant workflow limitation
Timezone handling in calendar apps remains a universal unsolved problem for travelers
The soft keyboard is inferior to physical keyboards for typing
money_quotes: The short statement is that I do like the htc magic android.
Like every other calendar app on the planet, Google calendar suffers from TimeZoneUncertainty.
I tried a Blackberry storm for a few days, but (how about this for irony) the email was no good for me.
I'm not allowing myself any such fun until I get the book finished.
key_insight: Early Android phones offered a viable alternative to iPhone for users locked into incompatible carrier plans, though they came with their own set of workflow limitations.
